food_subgroup: Vegetable curry / spiced wok-cooked vegetable dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Kadai Veg; closest defensible match is 'vegetable curry (food product)' FOODON_03311069. Kadai Veg is a specific North Indian wok-cooked spiced mixed vegetable preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Vegetable dishes) is the closest defensible category. No specific Kadai Veg entry exists in FoodEx2.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (capsicum, tomato, onion, carrot, cauliflower, peas)
kadai masala (coriander seeds, dried red chilli, cumin, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kadai Veg is approximately 300 g, representing one main-course portion as served in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Curry — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), composite mixed vegetable curry entry; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central reference values for mixed vegetable curry (FDC ID 169248) for micronutrient estimation.
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Kadai Veg entry exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry entry (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central mixed vegetable curry data. Kadai Veg is a tomato-and-spice-based wok-cooked preparation; macros are consistent with a medium-oil Indian vegetable curry. Micronutrients reported as -1 where neither source provides reliable Kadai-specific data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable curry — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable curry and spiced mixed vegetable dishes; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kadai Veg. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed vegetable curry (GI ~40–50, low category) and is consistent with the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables for spiced vegetable dishes. The low GI reflects the high fibre content of mixed vegetables, the acidity of the tomato-based sauce, and the moderate fat content which slows gastric emptying. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 21.3) / 100 = 9.6 (serving = 300 g, carbohydrate = 7.1 g/100 g × 300 g = 21.3 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~930 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Kadai Veg contributes approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 150–200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content (~7.1 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kadai Veg has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of ~9.6 per 300 g serving, making it a suitable main-course option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.6 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high vegetable fibre, and antioxidant-rich spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kadai Veg is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, with a fibre content of ~2.1 g/100 g and antioxidant-rich spices including turmeric and coriander; it is a heart-friendly choice, though the sodium content warrants attention in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kadai Veg (also spelled Kadhai Veg or Karahi Veg) is a popular North Indian vegetable dish cooked in a kadai, a traditional thick, circular, and deep cooking vessel resembling a wok. The dish features a colourful medley of mixed vegetables — typically capsicum (bell pepper), tomato, onion, cauliflower, carrot, and green peas — cooked in a robust, spiced tomato-based gravy. The defining flavour profile comes from freshly ground kadai masala, a spice blend of dry-roasted coriander seeds, dried red chillies, cumin, and garam masala, which imparts a bold, smoky, and slightly tangy character. Ginger and garlic are sautéed in oil before the vegetables and tomato gravy are added, and the dish is finished with fresh coriander leaves. The texture is semi-dry to semi-gravy, with vegetables retaining a slight bite. Kadai Veg is entirely plant-based, making it suitable for vegetarian and vegan diners. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~72 kcal/100 g) with a favourable macronutrient profile: low in saturated fat (~0.6 g/100 g), cholesterol-free, and providing dietary fibre (~2.1 g/100 g) from the mixed vegetables. Its estimated glycemic index of ~45 places it in the low-GI category, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) is the primary clinical consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, Kadai Veg is served in Indian vegetarian and North Indian restaurants, typically accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice, and is priced between SGD 11 and SGD 20 at restaurants such as Jewel of India, Anjana Kitchen, Indian House/Dumpukht, and Zaffron Tandoori.

confidence_reason: No exact Kadai Veg entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry entry (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed vegetable curry. Confidence is medium because the source match is adapted rather than exact.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry data (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central mixed vegetable curry reference; no exact Kadai Veg entry was found in any approved primary source. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for a plant-oil-cooked vegetable dish with no hydrogenated fats; this is a naturally zero value consistent with the ingredient profile. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal products. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as Kadai Veg is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per nutritional expectation for plant-based foods. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this food category; plant-based vegetable dishes do not contain meaningful vitamin D unless fortified. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for mixed vegetable curry and is not reliably available for this food category. 7. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for Kadai Veg has not been published. 8. Sodium content is estimated from typical restaurant preparation; home-cooked versions may have significantly lower sodium. 9. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate for a typical Indian restaurant main-course portion in Singapore. 10. Dietary flags: is_vegan and is_vegetarian are set to true based on the standard Kadai Veg formulation (no dairy, no meat); restaurant versions may occasionally include ghee or cream, which would change these flags.
